sanskrit_devanagari:
कर्मेन्द्रियाणि संयम्य य आस्ते मनसा स्मरन् ।
इन्द्रियार्थान्विमूढात्मा मिथ्याचारः स उच्यते ॥ ६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
karmendriyāṇi saṁyamya ya āste manasā smaran |
indriyārthān vimūḍhātmā mithyācāraḥ sa ucyate || 6 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: One who restrains the senses of action but whose mind dwells on sense objects certainly deludes himself and is called a pretender.
One who restrains the senses of action but whose mind dwells on sense objects certainly deludes himself and is called a pretender.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 940 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There are many pretenders who refuse to work in Kṛṣṇa consciousness but make a show of meditation, while actually dwelling within the mind upon sense enjoyment. Such pretenders may also speak on dry philosophy in order to bluff sophisticated followers, but according to this verse these are the great…

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.6 Yah, one who; samyamya, after withdrawing; karma-indriyani, the organs of action-hands etc.; aste, sits; manasa, mentally; smaran, recollecting, thinking; indriya-arthan, the objects of the senses; sah, that one; vimudha-atma, of deluded mind; ucyate, is called; mithya-acarah, a hypocrite, a sin…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-6-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Advaita reading is direct: 'yaḥ saṁyamya karma-indriyāṇi āste manasā smaran indriya-arthān, sah vimūḍha-ātmā, ucyate mithyā-ācāraḥ — a hypocrite, a sinful person.' The verse names the failure mode and judges it severely. The hypocrite is not merely confused; he is committing a real spiritual offense — his outward conduct contradicts his inner state.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.6 He whose inner and outer organs of senses are not conquered because of his sins not being annulled but is none the less struggling for winning knowledge of the self, whose mind is forced to turn away from the self by reason of it being attached to sense objects, and who consequently lets his min…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-6-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Vishishtadvaita diagnosis: the seeker whose inner and outer organs are not yet conquered (sins not annulled), but who nonetheless struggles for self-knowledge — his mind is forced to turn toward sense-objects; his actions belie his professions. He becomes 'perverted' by attempting jñāna-niṣṭhā prematurely. The verse names the consequence of skipping karma-yoga: the would-be jñāna-yogin without purification is a hypocrite, not because he is dishonest, but because his structure is misaligned.

positions.rendering: hypocrite, because his actions are at variance with his professions; he becomes perverted

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four traditions render mithyācāra hard. mithyā = false; ācāra = conduct. Both ācāryas use 'hypocrite' explicitly. The Advaita reading additionally calls him 'sinful person' (saintly = the inverse); the Vishishtadvaita reading adds the structural diagnosis: profession ≠ action, leading to spiritual perversion. Prabhupada's 'pretender' is somewhat softer than Mukundananda's 'hypocrites' or the ācāryas' direct rendering. Engineering layers must preserve the directness — the verse is not consoling but diagnostic.

literal_meaning: One who restrains the action-organs while sitting and mentally remembering the objects of the senses — that one of deluded self is called a hypocrite (mithyācāra).
traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.6 names a sharp pathology. The one who restrains the action-organs (karmendriyāṇi saṁyamya) while sitting and mentally remembering (manasā smaran) the objects of the senses — that vimūḍha-ātmā (deluded self) is mithyācāra, hypocrite. Per the Advaita reading: the gloss is direct — sah ucyate mithyācāraḥ, sinful person of false conduct. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: this is the seeker whose inner organ is not subdued, whose mind drifts to objects while his external posture mimics restraint; his ācāra (conduct) belies his profession. The verse names a recognizable failure mode: surface withdrawal coupled with interior agitation.
